responses.strategic_rationale: This parasomnia research unit operates at the intersection of three core tensions: (1) ecological validity versus safety—the residential environment must feel naturalistic to capture authentic behavior, yet infrastructure modifications and staff monitoring create clinical intrusion; (2) data resolution versus participant burden—capturing rare events requires extended stays and frequent enhanced-night PSG, but excessive monitoring may suppress the spontaneous behavior being studied; (3) scientific rigor versus operational scalability—dual-rater annotation and escalation-night protocols ensure methodological soundness, but manual review bottlenecks threaten sustainable throughput. The selected levers address these tensions by forcing explicit choices about environment design, event-capture strategy, staffing models, algorithm development scope, data governance, participant selection, and facility expansion timing—each lever clarifies a distinct decision boundary that shapes operational feasibility and scientific validity.

levers.name: Residential Environment Realism versus Clinical Control
levers.consequences: The balance between domestic atmosphere and systematic monitoring directly affects whether naturalistic parasomnia behavior emerges and whether staff can safely intervene during events. Excessive clinical infrastructure (visible cameras, overhead lighting, constant technician presence) may suppress episodes or produce atypical patterns unrepresentative of home behavior, undermining Aim 2's longitudinal phenotyping. Conversely, prioritizing comfort over safety creates liability risk and potentially inadequate event documentation.
options: Minimize visible monitoring infrastructure and staffing presence by relying primarily on discreet mattress sensors, ambient audio, and infrared cameras mounted unobtrusively in corner fixtures, with technician rounds only at shift change and on escalation-trigger nights, accepting reduced real-time oversight and delayed intervention capability.

Implement a standard clinical sleep laboratory aesthetic with bedside equipment, visible electrode arrays, intercom systems, and frequent technician check-ins during night hours, maximizing safety and event-capture responsiveness at the cost of potential suppression of naturalistic behavior and lower participant subjective comfort ratings.

Adopt a hotel-room hybrid model with concealed sensor arrays integrated into mattress and bedframe, discrete wall-mounted cameras positioned as ordinary room fixtures, and a technician stationed in an adjacent suite with audio monitoring and rapid-access protocols, balancing ecological realism with managed safety and event-response time.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between ecological validity and operational safety, but the options underestimate how participant habituation to environment occurs over 2–8 week stays—initial suppression effects may diminish substantially by week three, yet the plan provides no adaptive timeline for environment adjustment or data stratification by habituation phase.

levers.name: Event-Capture Frequency: Scheduled Enhanced Nights versus Extended Continuous Monitoring
levers.consequences: The current plan uses three fixed enhanced-night PSG sessions per participant (first, mid-stay, final) plus escalation nights after technician flags, optimizing cost-per-night while accepting the risk that episodes cluster unpredictably and many will be missed or captured only via low-burden sensors. Conversely, continuous full-montage PSG for all nights eliminates miss-rate risk but increases personnel demands, data volume, and annotation workload by approximately 3–4 fold, pushing throughput below the stated 30–35 participant target.
options: Maintain the fixed three-night enhanced PSG schedule supplemented by automatic escalation to full montage on nights when mattress-sensor thresholds or technician observation flags probable events, accepting that 25–35% of captured episodes may remain characterized at sensor-only resolution and requiring explicit discussion of resolution limitations in phenotyping publications.

Deploy full-montage PSG every night for all participants from arrival through week two, then switch to low-burden sensors for weeks three onward, front-loading the labor-intensive clinical-grade recording while episodic yield is highest and adjusting participant stay duration down to 3–5 weeks to maintain throughput.

Implement a dynamic adaptive protocol where participants with high episode frequency (≥4 flagged events per first week) receive continuous full PSG, while lower-frequency participants receive the fixed three-night schedule, allowing stratified resource allocation but introducing complex mid-stay protocol modifications and requiring prospectively validated frequency thresholds.
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses the tension between data granularity and operational sustainability, but none of the options adequately handle the inference problem: episodes captured at sensor-only resolution cannot reliably differentiate parasomnia subtypes (confusional arousal versus sleep terror) without post-hoc full PSG, potentially compromising Aim 2's morphological characterization across the full cohort.

levers.name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Scope: Ranking Tool versus Phenotyping-Ready Classifiers
levers.consequences: Aim 3 specifies building a triage tool that ranks probable episodes for human review, reducing annotation burden without claiming autonomous diagnosis. A narrow scope—a ranking model evaluated solely against technician flagging speed—is achievable with moderate computational effort and minimal false-positive risk. Conversely, developing subtype classifiers (sleep terror versus confusional arousal versus sleepwalking) that feed directly into phenotyping statistics risks overstating classification confidence and requires substantially larger labeled training sets and external validation.
options: Build a simple ranking model trained on low-burden sensor features and PSG indicators that sorts potential events by likelihood of being genuine parasomnia activity, with human scorers then adjudicating ranked candidates in order, reporting only sensitivity and specificity for event-presence detection and explicitly refraining from subtype classification claims.

Develop multi-class neural-network classifiers that attempt to assign each detected event to a parasomnia subtype (NREM stage-dependent confusional arousal, sleep terror with autonomic surge, motorized sleepwalking) using full-montage PSG, context (time in sleep cycle, prior arousal history), and sensor patterns, validated against dual-rater subtype agreement and published in methods papers as a contribution to parasomnia nosology.

Engineer a two-stage system where stage one ranks event likelihood as above, then stage two applies rule-based heuristics (motor intensity thresholds, EMG patterns, autonomic markers) to suggest subtype for technician review without claiming autonomous classification, reporting stage-two accuracy only on full-PSG nights and flagging low-confidence instances for manual consensus.
levers.review_lever: This lever centers on the tension between algorithmic ambition and evidence-grounded claims, but all three options sidestep a deeper problem: the ground-truth labels (dual human scorer agreement) may themselves be inconsistent for borderline cases, and the plan does not specify inter-rater kappa targets or how to handle rater disagreement in the model training pipeline.

levers.name: Staffing Model: Dedicated Night-Shift Technician Continuity versus Rotating Cross-Trained Pool
levers.consequences: Three technicians rotating nights to cover 8 suites at launch provides flexibility and reduces burnout but introduces inconsistency in event flagging thresholds, interpretation of video-sensor patterns, and real-time annotation quality—subtle between-technician variance directly propagates to Aim 2's phenotyping accuracy and Aim 3's algorithmic training. Alternatively, recruiting fewer full-time permanent night technicians at higher pay creates stronger institutional knowledge and protocol fidelity, but limits schedule flexibility and increases cost-per-night if technician utilization is lower during periods of low enrollment.
options: Hire three full-time research technicians rotating 4-night blocks with shared on-call backup, implement mandatory monthly cross-validation sessions where all technicians independently score the same 10 archived events and report individual-rater kappa scores to calibrate detection thresholds, and stratify Aim 3 algorithm development by technician to quantify between-rater variance explicitly.

Recruit two senior permanent night technicians (permanent contract, higher salary) supplemented by shift-hire agency staff or university graduate students during peak enrollment periods, prioritizing continuity for core protocol elements (enhanced-night setup, escalation-event capture, real-time annotation) while using flexible staff for routine monitoring and administrative tasks.

Establish a blended model with one PI-level senior technician (full-time, protocol authority) managing 8-suite coverage via remote real-time video monitoring with automated alert escalation, supported by two rotating associate technicians who handle in-person bedside tasks and physical safety checks, reducing headcount while concentrating expertise in event interpretation at the cost of higher technician burnout risk for the senior role.
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses the tension between data consistency and operational cost, but overlooks a critical dependency: the plan assumes technicians will flag events accurately in real time for escalation-night decisions, yet provides no baseline data on technician inter-rater reliability or the sensitivity/specificity of technician-driven triage, making it impossible to weight technician continuity value against cost.

levers.name: Participant Stay Length and Extension Protocols: Fixed Duration versus Event-Yield Adaptive Extension
levers.consequences: The current plan caps stays at 10 weeks with a protocol trigger (20% unproductive admissions) for criteria review, attempting to balance episode capture yield against participant burden and operational throughput. Shorter fixed stays (2–4 weeks) reduce costs and allow more participants, but guarantee a higher miss rate for low-frequency episodic individuals and may inflate reported non-capture rates artificially. Conversely, longer default stays (6–10 weeks from outset) increase per-participant costs and reduce throughput, but improve episode ascertainment and allow better characterization of temporal clustering patterns within individuals.
options: Set a fixed 4-week baseline stay for all NREM parasomnia participants regardless of event yield, with individual optional extension to 8 weeks available only to participants with zero or one captured event by week four who consent to continued stay, funding the extensions from a separate discretionary pool capped at 20% of cohort.  Track non-capture rates openly but do not trigger criteria review unless unproductive rates exceed 35% across rolling cohorts of ten.

Implement adaptive stay length determined by weekly event-yield assessment: participants with ≥3 captured events by week three transition to week-four discharge, while those with zero to two events extend automatically to week six; further extension to week ten is offered only if the participant remains highly motivated and capture pace has resumed, creating a naturalistic attrition curve and front-loading data from high-yield individuals.

Standardize all NREM parasomnia participants at an 8-week stay with one mid-stay discharge option (week four) only for those reporting that the residential environment triggered significant lifestyle burden or psychiatric distress, and use the extended window to build longitudinal within-person clustering analysis as a core Aim 2 deliverable, accepting reduced throughput (approximately 20–25 participants year two) in exchange for deeper phenotyping depth.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the trade-off between cohort size and individual phenotyping depth, but the options do not account for potential non-random dropout or the possibility that participants with lower-frequency parasomnias are also those at higher risk of early withdrawal, introducing selection bias that could distort Aim 2's between-person comparisons.

levers.name: Data Sharing Governance: Immediate De-identification and Open Deposit versus Conditional Access Controls
levers.consequences: The plan specifies de-identified physiological and sensor data for open repository deposit under data use agreements, while withholding bedroom video due to privacy constraints. This approach maximizes downstream research utility and transparency but requires robust de-identification procedures and carries residual re-identification risk if sensor or physiological patterns are highly distinctive. Stricter governance (conditional access, institutional-login-only repositories, or fee-based access) increases privacy protection and institutional control, but substantially reduces secondary research uptake and delays broader scientific validation of findings.
options: Deposit all de-identified physiological data (EEG, EOG, EMG, sleep stage labels, annotated event timestamps) in an open BIDS repository under Creative Commons licensing, de-identify sensor and video data by removing temporal identifiers and sequence information, and publish a detailed de-identification report including residual re-identification risk assessment and recommend that secondary analysts apply additional privacy-preserving analysis techniques when handling high-dimensional sensor features.

Implement a two-tier repository: physiology and preprocessed sleep metrics available immediately in open access, while raw sensor time-series (mattress pressure, room audio spectrograms, infrared depth imagery) deposited only in a controlled-access portal requiring signed data use agreements, proof of institutional affiliation, and PI approval of analysis plans, balancing openness with granular control over behavioral and environmental data.

Restrict all datasets to institutional partners and named collaborators via a managed-access portal under confidentiality agreements, with annual public release only of aggregate phenotypic statistics and algorithm performance benchmarks; retain raw physiological data and sensor time-series under institutional stewardship for internal validation and future replication studies, prioritizing long-term data integrity and reducing downstream liability risk.
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses the tension between scientific transparency and privacy stewardship, but the plan does not specify the technical criteria for de-identification (e.g., which sensor channels pose re-identification risk, what time-scale modifications are applied) or the governance procedures for handling re-identification requests, leaving ambiguity around actual data protection coverage.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Trigger: Pilot-Validation-Gated Growth versus Opportunistic Early Scaling
levers.consequences: The plan defers expansion to 12 suites until pilot data (24 months) demonstrate acceptable data quality, false-alarm rates, and annotation throughput. This gating strategy protects against scaling operational failures and ensures methods are validated before larger investment, but risks losing recruitment momentum, failing to meet the 50–70 participant enrollment target, and delaying publication timelines. Conversely, parallel expansion during pilot (8→12 suites by month 12) accelerates enrollment and increases event capture volume, but risks operational strain if staffing, annotation, or safety protocols prove inadequate at higher volume.
options: Maintain the 8-suite facility through month 18, require explicit external advisory board sign-off (inter-rater kappa ≥0.75 on events, false-alarm rate <15%, annotation backlog <2 weeks) before authorizing expansion planning, then proceed to 12 suites by month 24 if criteria are met, accepting the possibility that final enrollment may fall short of 50–70 if pilot-to-expansion transition is delayed.

Launch a modular expansion feasibility study starting month 9 in a second property (renting or leasing space adjacent or nearby), staffed by one additional technician, operating 4 suites with identical protocols to establish parallel operational proof-of-concept, allowing month 12–24 dual-site comparison and data-driven decision on whether full 12-suite expansion is operationally safe.

Commit to gradual site-based scaling: expand the current Bonn facility to 12 suites by month 15 using the existing renovation framework and hiring additional technicians in anticipation of increased enrollment, paralleling recruitment ramp-up and accepting operational risk in exchange for reaching the 50–70 enrollment target and positioning the facility for post-project sustainability and potential replication in a second German city.
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses the tension between prudent pilot-stage validation and growth momentum, but all options underestimate the non-linear scaling of inter-rater annotation burden—doubling suite capacity does not double throughput if the same independent scorers are required, and the plan specifies no contingency for hiring or training additional raters if expansion proceeds.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates three fundamental tensions: (1) ecological validity versus safety—the unit must feel domestic enough to elicit naturalistic sleep behavior while maintaining rigorous containment; (2) event rarity versus operational efficiency—infrequent episodes demand extended stays and continuous sensing, but long occupancy strains capacity and budget; (3) scientific rigor versus tool pragmatism—the event-triage system must reduce manual burden without over-claiming autonomous capability. These levers prioritize decisions that expose hidden costs, force trade-offs between fidelity and scale, and clarify what the facility can realistically deliver within resource constraints rather than optimizing any single dimension in isolation.

levers.name: Pre-Admission Screening Stringency: Clinic-Confirmed Phenotype versus Symptom Self-Report Enrollment
levers.consequences: Stricter screening (clinic confirmation, bed-partner collateral, prior PSG) reduces false negatives and facility admissions of participants unlikely to produce events, improving event yield and publication robustness. Conversely, relaxed criteria (accepting strong symptom reports without prior testing) accelerates recruitment and fills capacity faster, reducing idle cost per suite but risking higher unproductive admissions and blunted event-capture success. The choice directly determines whether the facility operates as a validation hub (requiring rigorous phenotyping entry) or a broad prospective cohort (accepting heterogeneous baseline data).
options: Require pre-admission structured clinical interview by the PI or trained sleep specialist, collateral history from bed partner, and documented prior or screening polysomnography before enrollment, accepting slower recruitment to maximize likelihood of at least one captured event per participant.

Accept referrals from sleep specialists with clinical diagnosis and self-reported episode frequency of at least 2 per month, bypassing formal pre-PSG testing, to accelerate enrollment and increase throughput while tolerating higher variability in phenotype clarity at entry.

Implement a two-tier entry pathway: high-confidence admissions (prior PSG confirmed, bed-partner corroboration) receive standard stay length, while unconfirmed referrals (specialist report only) undergo a brief in-lab screening night before committing to full residency, extending total time-to-data but segregating risk.
levers.review_lever: This lever embodies the tension between phenotypic purity and recruitment momentum, but the options do not address the hidden cost: over-screening increases screening burden on the University Hospital sleep clinic and may damage referral relationships if external neurologists perceive excessive gatekeeping. None of the options discuss how to communicate enrollment criteria transparently without creating referral friction.

levers.name: Sensor Escalation Timing: Real-Time Technician Detection versus Retrospective Event Nomination
levers.consequences: Real-time technician flagging during the night permits immediate PSG escalation, capturing recurrence at full clinical resolution and reducing lag between event observation and physiological documentation. However, it demands high technician acuity, raises false-alarm rates (especially early in training), and generates cost from redundant PSG nights on misclassified sleep movements. Retrospective nomination (technician reviews dry-sensor and camera footage the next morning, selecting nights for delayed PSG scoring) lowers annotation cost and false alarms but forfeits the opportunity to capture same-night recurrence and risks losing time-critical physiological context if events cluster within hours.
options: Deploy real-time event detection: technician actively monitors dry-EEG and infrared video during the night, calls escalation PSG within 30 minutes of flagged movement cluster or arousal, ensuring clinical-grade capture if recurrence occurs but accepting higher false-positive escalation cost and technician fatigue management.

Conduct end-of-shift retrospective review: technician annotates all low-burden sensor streams and video footage each morning, retrospectively nominates probable events, and schedules PSG scoring on subsequent nights for high-confidence nominations, reducing false alarms and nightly equipment burden but delaying PSG capture and risking loss of clustered recurrence.

Implement hybrid escalation with acuity tiers: low-confidence flagged events (isolated movement) undergo retrospective review the next morning; high-confidence flagged events (prolonged arousal cluster with complex motor behavior observed on video) trigger immediate PSG within 30 minutes, balancing response speed against false-alarm cost.
levers.review_lever: This lever trades speed-of-capture against annotation cost and technician workload, but overlooks a critical operational tension: technician fatigue and decision accuracy degrade overnight, especially in the first weeks before pattern recognition matures. None of the options address technician training curves, fatigue rotation, or how to validate that real-time flags are truly higher-fidelity than retrospective review after the night technician has rested.

levers.name: Stay Length Protocol: Standardized Duration versus Individualized Event-Yield Targeting
levers.consequences: Fixed stay length (e.g., 2 weeks for all participants regardless of event capture) simplifies scheduling, reduces administrative overhead, and produces consistent phenotyping windows across the cohort—valuable for between-person comparisons. However, it wastes facility capacity when early-admitting participants produce events within days (occupying suite unnecessarily) and yields incomplete data for low-frequency episodic cases who need 6–8 weeks to generate sufficient events for reliable characterization. Adaptive extension (extend up to 2 additional weeks for zero-event participants) increases per-participant data quality but creates scheduling fragility, increases per-event cost due to low-yield participants occupying suites longer, and complicates recruitment logistics if potential participants see indefinite stays as barriers.
options: Assign a uniform 14-day residency for all NREM parasomnia participants regardless of event capture within the stay, with optional post-visit remote monitoring via headband to retain longitudinal context; simplifies operations and produces comparable baseline data but tolerates unproductive admissions as a statistical cost of phenotyping diversity.

Implement tiered adaptive protocols: participants with zero events by day 10 are offered extension decisions daily based on self-reported sleep confidence and clinical judgment, with clear protocol ceiling of 10 weeks total; permits individualized data maximization but requires daily staff discretion and participant consent renegotiation.

Adopt a hybrid fixed-extension model: all participants receive 2 weeks baseline; those with zero events receive one mandatory offer of a 2-week extension (capped at 4 weeks total), with extension acceptance entered prospectively at day-10 decision point to allow recruitment planning; reduces administrative volatility while permitting event-yield optimization for a subset.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between operational consistency and scientific data completeness, but the options underestimate the human burden: extended stays strain participant psychological resilience, increase attrition risk if stays exceed 4 weeks, and may worsen data quality in fatigued or demotivated participants. None of the options address participant adherence or morale degradation during long admissions.

levers.name: Technician Annotation Authority: Final Event Adjudication versus Dual-Rater Verification Gate
levers.consequences: Granting night technicians final event annotation authority (with dual-rater review as secondary audit) accelerates real-time decision-making, reduces next-day overhead, and empowers technicians to flag escalation PSG immediately based on their judgment. However, technician expertise variability introduces systematic bias early in data collection, and errors in event classification propagate to the event-triage tool training set, risking biased algorithm development. Strict dual-rater verification (all events scored by two independent raters offline, with technician flagging as input only) ensures methodological rigor and defensible scoring but delays event confirmation by 1–2 days, loses the opportunity for same-night PSG escalation, and increases cost and bottleneck if raters are bottlenecked.
options: Designate night technicians as primary event scorers with authority to flag escalation PSG; institute weekly inter-rater reliability spot-checks (dual-rater review of 10% of flagged events) to monitor technician drift, adjusting training and criteria as needed; privileges real-time responsiveness over pre-event rigor but requires robust technician competency management.

Enforce mandatory dual-rater consensus on all events before escalation PSG or final event classification; technician reports observations only, two independent offline raters provide scored judgment, and escalation is triggered only after rater agreement; guarantees methodological consistency but forfeits real-time escalation capability and requires sufficient rater availability.

Stratify rater authority by event confidence: technician high-confidence flags (clear motor cluster with arousal signature on dry-EEG and prolonged video movement) permit immediate escalation; borderline or ambiguous flags undergo dual-rater review before escalation decision; combines real-time responsiveness for unambiguous events with methodological gatekeeping for uncertain cases.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the boundary between operational expediency and methodological defensibility, but the options sidestep a deeper issue: technician expertise takes 3–6 months to mature, yet the facility must make escalation decisions from week one. None of the options propose ramp-up protocols, supervised learning periods, or explicit competency milestones before technicians gain independent judgment.

levers.name: Control Cohort Recruitment Scope: Matched Pairs versus Broader Population Sample
levers.consequences: Matched controls (age, sex, body mass, sleep schedule similar to cases) maximize statistical power for within-pair event-triage benchmarking and provide tightly controlled baseline false-positive rates for sensor validation. However, intensive matching slows control recruitment, constrains the external generalizability of sensor false-alarm baselines to broader populations, and ties control numbers to case availability. Broader population controls (unmatched, recruited independently to represent general adult sleep patterns) accelerate control accrual, reduce recruitment dependencies, and improve generalizability of false-alarm benchmarks to future real-world deployment—but introduce confounding variability (sleep-apnea prevalence, age spread, body size heterogeneity) that complicates event-triage tool training if the algorithm cannot distinguish control baseline variation from true parasomnia signal.
options: Recruit matched controls: for each enrolled NREM parasomnia participant, enroll one control matched on age (±5 years), sex, and body mass index (±3 kg/m²), monitoring for 2 weeks in identical residential conditions; maximizes power for within-pair sensor false-alarm benchmarking but extends recruitment timeline and risks incomplete matching if control availability lags case enrollment.

Enroll a broad unmatched control cohort (target 20–25 controls, representative of adult ages 25–60, both sexes, unrestricted BMI) recruited independently from sleep clinic outpatients and community via Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin; accelerates control accrual and improves external generalizability of sensor baseline norms but increases confounding variability in arousal patterns and false-positive rates.

Implement stratified control recruitment with partial matching: controls are stratified by age decile and sex, with recruitment targets of 2–3 controls per stratum (n=20–30 total), ensuring age and sex coverage without strict pair-wise matching; balances recruitment agility with adequate stratification for downstream algorithmic control by case characteristics.
levers.review_lever: This lever trades statistical purity against recruitment efficiency and generalizability, but the options miss the methodological reality: if control nocturnal arousal patterns are confounded by undiagnosed sleep apnea or circadian disorders, the event-triage tool will learn spurious signal-to-noise discrimination. None of the options discuss screening controls for occult sleep pathology or handling controls with incidental findings.

levers.name: Nightly Sensor Burden Distribution: Continuous Baseline Acquisition versus Scheduled Targeted Nights
levers.consequences: Continuous baseline acquisition (dry-EEG, mattress sensor, camera, microphone running every night for all participants) provides uninterrupted context for within-person variability, temporal clustering of events, and sleep-stage transitions, supporting rich longitudinal phenotyping. However, continuous deployment increases data volume (requiring larger storage, faster backup pipelines, longer annotation time), raises participant burden and privacy concerns (continuous video recording), and demands robust equipment maintenance and battery management across all 8 suites. Scheduled targeted nights (baseline sensors run only 2–3 nights per week, escalated to nightly after event detection) reduces data burden and privacy footprint but sacrifices the granular temporal map of episodic clustering, slows detection of rare multi-night patterns, and may miss events on non-monitoring nights.
options: Deploy dry-EEG, contact-free mattress sensors, ambient infrared camera, and microphone every night for all active participants for the entire stay duration, accepting continuous data volume and privacy implications; guarantees maximal within-person temporal coverage and supports rich clustering analysis but requires robust nightly annotation workflow and participant comfort with continuous ambient monitoring.

Operate baseline sensors (dry-EEG, mattress, microphone, ambient camera) on a rotating schedule of 3 nights per week per participant, with accelerated nightly monitoring triggered after technician-flagged event; reduces continuous data volume and privacy intrusiveness but risks missing rare multi-night patterns and introduces selection bias if events cluster on scheduled monitoring nights by chance.

Run dry-EEG and contact-free mattress sensors nightly for arousal and movement context, but limit infrared camera and microphone to 2–3 scheduled nights per week (increased to nightly after event detection) to reduce video privacy burden while maintaining physiological baseline; balances physiological continuity against privacy and annotation overhead.
levers.review_lever: This lever trades continuous phenotypic fidelity against data management cost and participant privacy comfort, but the options do not address the hidden trade-off: selective monitoring nights may systematically over-detect events on filmed nights if participants are more hypervigilant when they know they are being recorded. None of the options propose blinding participants to monitoring schedules or assessing reactivity.

levers.name: Post-Study Data Retention and Ancillary Biospecimen Collection: Lean Repository versus Enriched Archive
levers.consequences: Lean repository approach (deposit only de-identified EEG and sensor streams in BIDS format; discard raw video; no biospecimens) minimizes storage cost, simplifies long-term legal obligations, and reduces participant re-consent burden for future secondary uses. However, it forgoes the opportunity to link parasomnia phenotypes to genetic, proteomic, or metabolic markers in future studies, limits richness of re-analysis if new automated scoring methods emerge, and reduces the facility's attractiveness to pharmaceutical partners or larger consortium efforts. Enriched archive (retain raw and processed EEG, video for approved secondary research, collect and bank saliva or blood for future biomarker studies) enables richer longitudinal science and cross-domain discovery but increases storage infrastructure cost, requires robust informed-consent language for future uses, and raises governance complexity if biospecimens are shared with external partners.
options: Adopt a minimalist data repository: de-identify and deposit only processed EEG montages and sensor streams in BIDS format to a recognized repository; retain raw video locally under access controls for a maximum of 5 years, then destroy; collect no biospecimens; minimizes storage, legal, and consent overhead but forecloses future biomarker studies and rich re-analysis.

Establish an enriched institutional archive: deposit de-identified processed EEG, raw and processed sensor streams, and secure de-identified video clips (key moments flagged by technician) in a university-controlled repository under conditional-access data use agreements; collect and bank saliva samples (DNA, proteins) for each participant with extended consent for genetic or metabolic studies; enables richer future research but requires governance infrastructure and long-term storage commitment.

Implement a hybrid archive with tiered retention: de-identify and publicly deposit processed EEG and sensor data in BIDS format; retain raw video and processed proprietary montages in a secure university repository with a 10-year retention mandate under restricted access agreements; collect saliva samples and bank with explicit participant consent for approved academic collaborators only, excluding commercial use without fresh consent.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between present scientific stewardship and future research opportunity, but the options do not adequately address the practical risk: biospecimen banking requires cryogenic infrastructure, regulatory compliance, and trained personnel—if these are absent at year one, building them later is costlier and slower. None of the options explicitly evaluate institutional capacity or discuss whether biobanking aligns with the facility's core mission of parasomnia phenotyping rather than becoming scope creep.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia unit must navigate fundamental tensions between ecological validity and operational safety, between event rarity and resource efficiency, and between data richness and privacy protection. The levers below target these core conflicts: how to design a capture environment that provokes natural sleep behavior while remaining safe; how to balance continuous monitoring costs against episodic event unpredictability; how to manage the 'long tail' of low-yield stays without inflating budget or compromising phenotyping depth; and how to build a sustainable staffing and annotation workflow when night-shift continuity directly affects both participant safety and data quality. These levers are critical because they determine whether the unit can sustain 24–36 months of operations, recruit and retain 50–70 participants, and generate the phenotyping and validation outputs required for phase-two funding.

levers.name: Sleep-Onset Context Manipulation: Pharmacological Sleep Induction versus Naturalistic Circadian Entrainment
levers.consequences: Using short-acting sedatives (e.g., low-dose zopiclone) on arrival nights accelerates sleep onset and shortens the time to observable REM and NREM architecture, potentially increasing early event capture. However, sedative use alters intrinsic arousal thresholds and parasomnia susceptibility, introducing a confound that complicates phenotyping and limits generalizability to unsedated home sleep. Strict circadian entrainment (dark adaptation, scheduled lights-off, no sedation) preserves naturalistic physiology but extends the pre-event observation window and increases length-of-stay costs for slow sleepers.
options: Administer standardized low-dose sedation on participant arrival night only, then transition to naturalistic sleep across remaining nights, documenting the sedation effect in all analyses as a controlled confounder

Implement a 3–5 day circadian entrainment protocol prior to formal monitoring, with early-arrival accommodation in the residential unit to allow sleep-wake rhythm synchronization without pharmacological intervention

Randomize incoming participants to sedated versus unsedated-onset groups prospectively, creating two matched cohorts to quantify the magnitude of sedation-induced sleep architecture changes and parasomnia expression
levers.review_lever: This lever balances ecological validity against capture speed, but the options do not address the practical ethics question: if sedation demonstrably increases early event yield, is withholding it from the unsedated group justifiable given limited recruitment capacity? None of the options propose transparent participant consent language that acknowledges this trade-off.

levers.name: Technician Staffing Schedule: Three-Person Continuous Coverage versus Singleton Night-Watch with On-Call Backup
levers.consequences: Three rotating technicians on each night ensure redundancy, rapid response to safety incidents, and real-time annotation of multiple simultaneous events across 8 suites. This model's overhead cost is approximately €180–200K annually in salary and social insurance. A singleton night-watch with on-call backup (two technicians covering three nights each per week, plus a rapid-response clinician on standby) reduces staffing costs by 35–40% but creates a single point of failure: a sole technician cannot simultaneously monitor safety, annotate events, and respond to medical episodes in multiple suites.
options: Deploy three rotating technicians every night across all 8 suites for the full 3-year operational period, accepting higher staffing cost in exchange for consistent safety margin and annotation throughput

Pilot a singleton-plus-on-call model for the first 12 months with early safety gates (incident reporting, event-miss audit, staff fatigue tracking), and escalate to three-person coverage if safety incidents exceed 2 per month or annotation delay exceeds 48 hours

Hire two full-time night technicians with a third contracted on-call position, staffed 5 nights per week with an on-site resident clinician (rotating MD or NP) available for clinical decisions on nights with lower expected occupancy
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the core tension between cost containment and safety culture, but the options overlook the long-term burnout risk: even rotating three technicians will face cumulative fatigue and turnover if the role lacks advancement pathways or continued education funding. None of the options propose how to retain skilled staff beyond 3 years or transition to a sustainable post-pilot staffing model.

levers.name: Annotation Workflow Authority: Single-Technician Real-Time Flagging versus Delayed Consensus Screening Panel
levers.consequences: Real-time annotation by the night technician (who flags candidate parasomnia events and triggers PSG escalation if criteria are met) enables immediate clinical response and reduced revisit burden, but introduces subjective bias and inter-technician variability in event nomination. A delayed consensus panel (two independent sleep medicine physicians reviewing video/PSG 24–48 hours post-recording and adjudicating flagged events via standardized rubric) guarantees AASM-criterion adherence but adds 2–5 days of latency, delaying PSG escalation and potentially missing recurrent events during the same stay.
options: Empower each night technician to independently nominate candidate parasomnia events in real time using a laminated decision tree, trigger PSG escalation immediately upon nomination, and conduct blinded dual-rater scoring 1–2 weeks post-stay for research adjudication

Establish a daily remote morning review by a designated sleep physician who reviews overnight video and sensor data flagged by the technician, makes same-day clinical escalation decisions, and assigns events to dual-rater scoring

Implement a hybrid model where technicians flag low-confidence candidate events for morning review by the PI or on-call clinician (turnaround <12 hours), while high-confidence events (e.g., clear video-documented complex motor behavior matching pre-study witness account) trigger immediate PSG escalation without waiting for physician review
levers.review_lever: This lever balances rapid clinical responsiveness against diagnostic rigor, but the options do not adequately address annotation burden escalation: as participant volume increases from 15–20 to 30–35, the number of flagged events will multiply, and dual-rater availability may become the bottleneck. None propose how to scale the dual-rater pool or integrate semi-automated pre-screening to reduce manual reviewer load.

levers.name: Early-Discharge Threshold for Low-Yield Participants: Hard Protocol Ceiling versus Extended Observation with Phenotypic Reassessment
levers.consequences: Discharging participants after 2 weeks without a captured event saves approximately €2,100 per participant in accommodation and technician time, preserving budget for additional enrollments. However, removing low-yield participants introduces selection bias (faster, shallower sleepers or those with genuinely infrequent episodes are overrepresented in the discharge group), skewing the longitudinal phenotyping cohort. The current protocol permits extension up to 10 weeks with potential for 20% unproductive admissions; stricter early-discharge policies reduce the unproductive allowance but risk losing participants whose episodes cluster later in the stay.
options: Establish an absolute 2-week maximum stay (or 3 weeks if one event has been captured) regardless of ongoing monitoring interest, with post-discharge phone follow-up at 1, 3, and 6 months to capture delayed phenotypic clustering and stratify the null-yield group by eventual symptom trajectory

Implement a staged protocol: 2 weeks of baseline monitoring followed by a blinded reassessment interview (with bed partner if available) to re-examine symptom frequency and triggers; if interview suggests ongoing high likelihood, extend stay for an additional 2–3 weeks with modified environmental or sleep-timing interventions targeted at participant's reported trigger patterns

Admit all participants with confirmed pre-admission parasomnia diagnosis for a fixed 4-week minimum stay, allowing at least two complete sleep cycles for potential within-person temporal clustering, with discharge extensions considered only after the 4-week gate is reached
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the trade-off between statistical efficiency and sample representativeness, but the options do not address the ethical implication: extending stays for low-yield participants may create expectancy pressure (both on staff and participants) for events to occur, subtly incentivizing over-interpretation of ambiguous arousal bursts as parasomnia candidates. None propose how to monitor and manage this form of subtle observer bias.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Scoring Protocol: Strict AASM Criterion Adherence versus Pragmatic Consensus Rubric
levers.consequences: AASM-criterion-only scoring (both raters independently apply published polysomnography scoring rules without modification) ensures external comparability and aligns with clinical standards, but the criteria were designed for single-night diagnostic PSG and may not account for within-person variability, recurrent subclinical arousal patterns, or subtle morphological differences that characterize longitudinal monitoring. A locally developed consensus rubric (co-authored by the two independent raters, documented in a technical supplement) can capture nuanced longitudinal phenomena and reduce false negatives but sacrifices generalizability and introduces the appearance of ad-hoc rule-making that may be challenged in peer review.
options: Score all 50–70 participants using strict published AASM criteria, with a separate exploratory analysis layer that documents borderline or ambiguous events flagged by either rater and summarizes their characteristics; report AASM-criterion adherence as the primary results and exploratory findings as supplementary

Develop a hybrid rubric during the pilot phase (first 15–20 participants) in collaboration with two independent sleep physicians external to the project, pre-register the rubric, and apply it uniformly to all 70 participants, with transparent documentation of where the rubric deviates from or extends published AASM guidance

Train both independent raters using didactic video examples (parasomnia vs. confusional arousal vs. normal arousal boundary cases) and conduct a structured calibration scoring session (10–15 blinded reference events) before they score study data, then report inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa and ICC) as the primary validity metric alongside event counts
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses the tension between standardization and fidelity, but the options overlook the reputational risk: if the study is later found to have departed from AASM criteria without preregistration, the entire dataset may be discounted by the field. None of the options propose prospective publication of the scoring protocol or early expert review by the AASM polysomnography task force.

levers.name: Sensor Failure and Data Loss Contingency: Redundant Recording Across All Participants versus Tiered Redundancy by Participant Risk Profile
levers.consequences: Recording all participants on dual independent sensor streams (e.g., two separate EEG headband units plus the PSG montage on enhanced nights) ensures no single hardware failure loses the physiological record, but doubles equipment cost (approximately €80–100K in headbands plus redundant storage) and increases data management complexity without increasing the probability of actual event capture (redundancy protects against sensor dropout, not against event rarity). Tiered redundancy (dual sensors only for participants with low baseline event frequency or those on extended stays; single sensors for high-yielding participants) reduces cost but introduces operational complexity and creates a per-participant decision point that may be biased by early event yield.
options: Deploy dual independent EEG headband systems on all participants from day one, synchronized via network time protocol, with automatic failover annotation in the data pipeline if one stream drops; report data completeness as a primary quality metric

Implement single-sensor baseline recording for all participants, but automatically escalate to dual-sensor backup recording for any participant whose first 5 nights produce zero flagged events, or whose initial event-capture rate falls below the cohort median by day 10

Assign redundancy status at admission based on pre-study frequency history (participants reporting <1 episode per month receive dual sensors; ≥1 per month receive single) with protocol-specified criteria for upgrades during the stay if low yield persists
levers.review_lever: This lever balances cost against data robustness, but the options do not address the subtle cognitive bias they introduce: staff may unconsciously invest more annotation effort in high-redundancy (high-risk) participants, creating false-negative bias in low-redundancy (presumed high-yield) participants. None of the options propose blinded annotation schemes or periodic random re-review of single-sensor data.

levers.name: Facility Capacity Activation Timing: Phased Expansion to 10 Suites at Month 12 versus Maintaining 8-Suite Steady State through Year Three
levers.consequences: Expanding from 8 to 10 active suites during year two requires approximately €150–180K in additional renovation, equipment, and one incremental technician FTE (offset by one additional participant per month generating €800 in annual revenue). Expansion shortens average time-to-event by 20–25% due to larger cohort size, but introduces operational risk: the pilot phase (first 12 months) may uncover staffing, safety, or annotation bottlenecks that cannot be corrected once the 10-suite footprint is activated. Remaining at 8 suites preserves budget flexibility, reduces operational complexity, and allows 50–60 participants over 3 years; expansion enables 65–75 participants but requires successful resolution of hidden year-one challenges.
options: Commit to 8-suite operations through month 36, using freed capital (€150K+) for enhanced technician training, redundant equipment, and a post-hoc validation cohort recruited in year three to test the semi-automated event-triage model on independent data

Expand to 10 suites at month 13 (after 12 months of pilot operations and external advisory board approval), contingent on meeting three go-live gates: zero serious safety incidents, participant enrollment on pace (≥12 cumulative admits), and annotation throughput sustainable within 48 hours

Execute a phased expansion: activate a 9th suite at month 12 with the incumbent technician team and concurrent hiring of one additional FTE; do not open the 10th suite unless event-capture rates remain above the pre-study assumption (70–80% with ≥1 event per stay) after 20 total admissions
levers.review_lever: This lever determines the long-term resource envelope and success probability, but the options do not adequately weigh the hidden costs of over-expansion: adding suites increases the minimum safe occupancy threshold required to justify staffing, which paradoxically makes recruitment pressure more acute if enrollment slows. None of the options propose how to right-size staffing dynamically if cohort size plateaus unexpectedly.

levers.name: Residential Environment Realism versus Clinical Control
levers.consequences: The balance between domestic atmosphere and systematic monitoring directly affects whether naturalistic parasomnia behavior emerges and whether staff can safely intervene during events. Excessive clinical infrastructure (visible cameras, overhead lighting, constant technician presence) may suppress episodes or produce atypical patterns unrepresentative of home behavior, undermining Aim 2's longitudinal phenotyping. Conversely, prioritizing comfort over safety creates liability risk and potentially inadequate event documentation.
options: Minimize visible monitoring infrastructure and staffing presence by relying primarily on discreet mattress sensors, ambient audio, and infrared cameras mounted unobtrusively in corner fixtures, with technician rounds only at shift change and on escalation-trigger nights, accepting reduced real-time oversight and delayed intervention capability.

Implement a standard clinical sleep laboratory aesthetic with bedside equipment, visible electrode arrays, intercom systems, and frequent technician check-ins during night hours, maximizing safety and event-capture responsiveness at the cost of potential suppression of naturalistic behavior and lower participant subjective comfort ratings.

Adopt a hotel-room hybrid model with concealed sensor arrays integrated into mattress and bedframe, discrete wall-mounted cameras positioned as ordinary room fixtures, and a technician stationed in an adjacent suite with audio monitoring and rapid-access protocols, balancing ecological realism with managed safety and event-response time.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between ecological validity and operational safety, but the options underestimate how participant habituation to environment occurs over 2–8 week stays—initial suppression effects may diminish substantially by week three, yet the plan provides no adaptive timeline for environment adjustment or data stratification by habituation phase.

levers.name: Event-Capture Frequency: Scheduled Enhanced Nights versus Extended Continuous Monitoring
levers.consequences: The current plan uses three fixed enhanced-night PSG sessions per participant (first, mid-stay, final) plus escalation nights after technician flags, optimizing cost-per-night while accepting the risk that episodes cluster unpredictably and many will be missed or captured only via low-burden sensors. Conversely, continuous full-montage PSG for all nights eliminates miss-rate risk but increases personnel demands, data volume, and annotation workload by approximately 3–4 fold, pushing throughput below the stated 30–35 participant target.
options: Maintain the fixed three-night enhanced PSG schedule supplemented by automatic escalation to full montage on nights when mattress-sensor thresholds or technician observation flags probable events, accepting that 25–35% of captured episodes may remain characterized at sensor-only resolution and requiring explicit discussion of resolution limitations in phenotyping publications.

Deploy full-montage PSG every night for all participants from arrival through week two, then switch to low-burden sensors for weeks three onward, front-loading the labor-intensive clinical-grade recording while episodic yield is highest and adjusting participant stay duration down to 3–5 weeks to maintain throughput.

Implement a dynamic adaptive protocol where participants with high episode frequency (≥4 flagged events per first week) receive continuous full PSG, while lower-frequency participants receive the fixed three-night schedule, allowing stratified resource allocation but introducing complex mid-stay protocol modifications and requiring prospectively validated frequency thresholds.
levers.review: This lever addresses the tension between data granularity and operational sustainability, but none of the options adequately handle the inference problem: episodes captured at sensor-only resolution cannot reliably differentiate parasomnia subtypes (confusional arousal versus sleep terror) without post-hoc full PSG, potentially compromising Aim 2's morphological characterization across the full cohort.

levers.name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Scope: Ranking Tool versus Phenotyping-Ready Classifiers
levers.consequences: Aim 3 specifies building a triage tool that ranks probable episodes for human review, reducing annotation burden without claiming autonomous diagnosis. A narrow scope—a ranking model evaluated solely against technician flagging speed—is achievable with moderate computational effort and minimal false-positive risk. Conversely, developing subtype classifiers (sleep terror versus confusional arousal versus sleepwalking) that feed directly into phenotyping statistics risks overstating classification confidence and requires substantially larger labeled training sets and external validation.
options: Build a simple ranking model trained on low-burden sensor features and PSG indicators that sorts potential events by likelihood of being genuine parasomnia activity, with human scorers then adjudicating ranked candidates in order, reporting only sensitivity and specificity for event-presence detection and explicitly refraining from subtype classification claims.

Develop multi-class neural-network classifiers that attempt to assign each detected event to a parasomnia subtype (NREM stage-dependent confusional arousal, sleep terror with autonomic surge, motorized sleepwalking) using full-montage PSG, context (time in sleep cycle, prior arousal history), and sensor patterns, validated against dual-rater subtype agreement and published in methods papers as a contribution to parasomnia nosology.

Engineer a two-stage system where stage one ranks event likelihood as above, then stage two applies rule-based heuristics (motor intensity thresholds, EMG patterns, autonomic markers) to suggest subtype for technician review without claiming autonomous classification, reporting stage-two accuracy only on full-PSG nights and flagging low-confidence instances for manual consensus.
levers.review: This lever centers on the tension between algorithmic ambition and evidence-grounded claims, but all three options sidestep a deeper problem: the ground-truth labels (dual human scorer agreement) may themselves be inconsistent for borderline cases, and the plan does not specify inter-rater kappa targets or how to handle rater disagreement in the model training pipeline.

levers.name: Staffing Model: Dedicated Night-Shift Technician Continuity versus Rotating Cross-Trained Pool
levers.consequences: Three technicians rotating nights to cover 8 suites at launch provides flexibility and reduces burnout but introduces inconsistency in event flagging thresholds, interpretation of video-sensor patterns, and real-time annotation quality—subtle between-technician variance directly propagates to Aim 2's phenotyping accuracy and Aim 3's algorithmic training. Alternatively, recruiting fewer full-time permanent night technicians at higher pay creates stronger institutional knowledge and protocol fidelity, but limits schedule flexibility and increases cost-per-night if technician utilization is lower during periods of low enrollment.
options: Hire three full-time research technicians rotating 4-night blocks with shared on-call backup, implement mandatory monthly cross-validation sessions where all technicians independently score the same 10 archived events and report individual-rater kappa scores to calibrate detection thresholds, and stratify Aim 3 algorithm development by technician to quantify between-rater variance explicitly.

Recruit two senior permanent night technicians (permanent contract, higher salary) supplemented by shift-hire agency staff or university graduate students during peak enrollment periods, prioritizing continuity for core protocol elements (enhanced-night setup, escalation-event capture, real-time annotation) while using flexible staff for routine monitoring and administrative tasks.

Establish a blended model with one PI-level senior technician (full-time, protocol authority) managing 8-suite coverage via remote real-time video monitoring with automated alert escalation, supported by two rotating associate technicians who handle in-person bedside tasks and physical safety checks, reducing headcount while concentrating expertise in event interpretation at the cost of higher technician burnout risk for the senior role.
levers.review: This lever addresses the tension between data consistency and operational cost, but overlooks a critical dependency: the plan assumes technicians will flag events accurately in real time for escalation-night decisions, yet provides no baseline data on technician inter-rater reliability or the sensitivity/specificity of technician-driven triage, making it impossible to weight technician continuity value against cost.

levers.name: Participant Stay Length and Extension Protocols: Fixed Duration versus Event-Yield Adaptive Extension
levers.consequences: The current plan caps stays at 10 weeks with a protocol trigger (20% unproductive admissions) for criteria review, attempting to balance episode capture yield against participant burden and operational throughput. Shorter fixed stays (2–4 weeks) reduce costs and allow more participants, but guarantee a higher miss rate for low-frequency episodic individuals and may inflate reported non-capture rates artificially. Conversely, longer default stays (6–10 weeks from outset) increase per-participant costs and reduce throughput, but improve episode ascertainment and allow better characterization of temporal clustering patterns within individuals.
options: Set a fixed 4-week baseline stay for all NREM parasomnia participants regardless of event yield, with individual optional extension to 8 weeks available only to participants with zero or one captured event by week four who consent to continued stay, funding the extensions from a separate discretionary pool capped at 20% of cohort.  Track non-capture rates openly but do not trigger criteria review unless unproductive rates exceed 35% across rolling cohorts of ten.

Implement adaptive stay length determined by weekly event-yield assessment: participants with ≥3 captured events by week three transition to week-four discharge, while those with zero to two events extend automatically to week six; further extension to week ten is offered only if the participant remains highly motivated and capture pace has resumed, creating a naturalistic attrition curve and front-loading data from high-yield individuals.

Standardize all NREM parasomnia participants at an 8-week stay with one mid-stay discharge option (week four) only for those reporting that the residential environment triggered significant lifestyle burden or psychiatric distress, and use the extended window to build longitudinal within-person clustering analysis as a core Aim 2 deliverable, accepting reduced throughput (approximately 20–25 participants year two) in exchange for deeper phenotyping depth.
levers.review: This lever governs the trade-off between cohort size and individual phenotyping depth, but the options do not account for potential non-random dropout or the possibility that participants with lower-frequency parasomnias are also those at higher risk of early withdrawal, introducing selection bias that could distort Aim 2's between-person comparisons.

levers.name: Data Sharing Governance: Immediate De-identification and Open Deposit versus Conditional Access Controls
levers.consequences: The plan specifies de-identified physiological and sensor data for open repository deposit under data use agreements, while withholding bedroom video due to privacy constraints. This approach maximizes downstream research utility and transparency but requires robust de-identification procedures and carries residual re-identification risk if sensor or physiological patterns are highly distinctive. Stricter governance (conditional access, institutional-login-only repositories, or fee-based access) increases privacy protection and institutional control, but substantially reduces secondary research uptake and delays broader scientific validation of findings.
options: Deposit all de-identified physiological data (EEG, EOG, EMG, sleep stage labels, annotated event timestamps) in an open BIDS repository under Creative Commons licensing, de-identify sensor and video data by removing temporal identifiers and sequence information, and publish a detailed de-identification report including residual re-identification risk assessment and recommend that secondary analysts apply additional privacy-preserving analysis techniques when handling high-dimensional sensor features.

Implement a two-tier repository: physiology and preprocessed sleep metrics available immediately in open access, while raw sensor time-series (mattress pressure, room audio spectrograms, infrared depth imagery) deposited only in a controlled-access portal requiring signed data use agreements, proof of institutional affiliation, and PI approval of analysis plans, balancing openness with granular control over behavioral and environmental data.

Restrict all datasets to institutional partners and named collaborators via a managed-access portal under confidentiality agreements, with annual public release only of aggregate phenotypic statistics and algorithm performance benchmarks; retain raw physiological data and sensor time-series under institutional stewardship for internal validation and future replication studies, prioritizing long-term data integrity and reducing downstream liability risk.
levers.review: This lever addresses the tension between scientific transparency and privacy stewardship, but the plan does not specify the technical criteria for de-identification (e.g., which sensor channels pose re-identification risk, what time-scale modifications are applied) or the governance procedures for handling re-identification requests, leaving ambiguity around actual data protection coverage.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Trigger: Pilot-Validation-Gated Growth versus Opportunistic Early Scaling
levers.consequences: The plan defers expansion to 12 suites until pilot data (24 months) demonstrate acceptable data quality, false-alarm rates, and annotation throughput. This gating strategy protects against scaling operational failures and ensures methods are validated before larger investment, but risks losing recruitment momentum, failing to meet the 50–70 participant enrollment target, and delaying publication timelines. Conversely, parallel expansion during pilot (8→12 suites by month 12) accelerates enrollment and increases event capture volume, but risks operational strain if staffing, annotation, or safety protocols prove inadequate at higher volume.
options: Maintain the 8-suite facility through month 18, require explicit external advisory board sign-off (inter-rater kappa ≥0.75 on events, false-alarm rate <15%, annotation backlog <2 weeks) before authorizing expansion planning, then proceed to 12 suites by month 24 if criteria are met, accepting the possibility that final enrollment may fall short of 50–70 if pilot-to-expansion transition is delayed.

Launch a modular expansion feasibility study starting month 9 in a second property (renting or leasing space adjacent or nearby), staffed by one additional technician, operating 4 suites with identical protocols to establish parallel operational proof-of-concept, allowing month 12–24 dual-site comparison and data-driven decision on whether full 12-suite expansion is operationally safe.

Commit to gradual site-based scaling: expand the current Bonn facility to 12 suites by month 15 using the existing renovation framework and hiring additional technicians in anticipation of increased enrollment, paralleling recruitment ramp-up and accepting operational risk in exchange for reaching the 50–70 enrollment target and positioning the facility for post-project sustainability and potential replication in a second German city.
levers.review: This lever addresses the tension between prudent pilot-stage validation and growth momentum, but all options underestimate the non-linear scaling of inter-rater annotation burden—doubling suite capacity does not double throughput if the same independent scorers are required, and the plan specifies no contingency for hiring or training additional raters if expansion proceeds.

levers.name: Pre-Admission Screening Stringency: Clinic-Confirmed Phenotype versus Symptom Self-Report Enrollment
levers.consequences: Stricter screening (clinic confirmation, bed-partner collateral, prior PSG) reduces false negatives and facility admissions of participants unlikely to produce events, improving event yield and publication robustness. Conversely, relaxed criteria (accepting strong symptom reports without prior testing) accelerates recruitment and fills capacity faster, reducing idle cost per suite but risking higher unproductive admissions and blunted event-capture success. The choice directly determines whether the facility operates as a validation hub (requiring rigorous phenotyping entry) or a broad prospective cohort (accepting heterogeneous baseline data).
options: Require pre-admission structured clinical interview by the PI or trained sleep specialist, collateral history from bed partner, and documented prior or screening polysomnography before enrollment, accepting slower recruitment to maximize likelihood of at least one captured event per participant.

Accept referrals from sleep specialists with clinical diagnosis and self-reported episode frequency of at least 2 per month, bypassing formal pre-PSG testing, to accelerate enrollment and increase throughput while tolerating higher variability in phenotype clarity at entry.

Implement a two-tier entry pathway: high-confidence admissions (prior PSG confirmed, bed-partner corroboration) receive standard stay length, while unconfirmed referrals (specialist report only) undergo a brief in-lab screening night before committing to full residency, extending total time-to-data but segregating risk.
levers.review: This lever embodies the tension between phenotypic purity and recruitment momentum, but the options do not address the hidden cost: over-screening increases screening burden on the University Hospital sleep clinic and may damage referral relationships if external neurologists perceive excessive gatekeeping. None of the options discuss how to communicate enrollment criteria transparently without creating referral friction.

levers.name: Sensor Escalation Timing: Real-Time Technician Detection versus Retrospective Event Nomination
levers.consequences: Real-time technician flagging during the night permits immediate PSG escalation, capturing recurrence at full clinical resolution and reducing lag between event observation and physiological documentation. However, it demands high technician acuity, raises false-alarm rates (especially early in training), and generates cost from redundant PSG nights on misclassified sleep movements. Retrospective nomination (technician reviews dry-sensor and camera footage the next morning, selecting nights for delayed PSG scoring) lowers annotation cost and false alarms but forfeits the opportunity to capture same-night recurrence and risks losing time-critical physiological context if events cluster within hours.
options: Deploy real-time event detection: technician actively monitors dry-EEG and infrared video during the night, calls escalation PSG within 30 minutes of flagged movement cluster or arousal, ensuring clinical-grade capture if recurrence occurs but accepting higher false-positive escalation cost and technician fatigue management.

Conduct end-of-shift retrospective review: technician annotates all low-burden sensor streams and video footage each morning, retrospectively nominates probable events, and schedules PSG scoring on subsequent nights for high-confidence nominations, reducing false alarms and nightly equipment burden but delaying PSG capture and risking loss of clustered recurrence.

Implement hybrid escalation with acuity tiers: low-confidence flagged events (isolated movement) undergo retrospective review the next morning; high-confidence flagged events (prolonged arousal cluster with complex motor behavior observed on video) trigger immediate PSG within 30 minutes, balancing response speed against false-alarm cost.
levers.review: This lever trades speed-of-capture against annotation cost and technician workload, but overlooks a critical operational tension: technician fatigue and decision accuracy degrade overnight, especially in the first weeks before pattern recognition matures. None of the options address technician training curves, fatigue rotation, or how to validate that real-time flags are truly higher-fidelity than retrospective review after the night technician has rested.

levers.name: Stay Length Protocol: Standardized Duration versus Individualized Event-Yield Targeting
levers.consequences: Fixed stay length (e.g., 2 weeks for all participants regardless of event capture) simplifies scheduling, reduces administrative overhead, and produces consistent phenotyping windows across the cohort—valuable for between-person comparisons. However, it wastes facility capacity when early-admitting participants produce events within days (occupying suite unnecessarily) and yields incomplete data for low-frequency episodic cases who need 6–8 weeks to generate sufficient events for reliable characterization. Adaptive extension (extend up to 2 additional weeks for zero-event participants) increases per-participant data quality but creates scheduling fragility, increases per-event cost due to low-yield participants occupying suites longer, and complicates recruitment logistics if potential participants see indefinite stays as barriers.
options: Assign a uniform 14-day residency for all NREM parasomnia participants regardless of event capture within the stay, with optional post-visit remote monitoring via headband to retain longitudinal context; simplifies operations and produces comparable baseline data but tolerates unproductive admissions as a statistical cost of phenotyping diversity.

Implement tiered adaptive protocols: participants with zero events by day 10 are offered extension decisions daily based on self-reported sleep confidence and clinical judgment, with clear protocol ceiling of 10 weeks total; permits individualized data maximization but requires daily staff discretion and participant consent renegotiation.

Adopt a hybrid fixed-extension model: all participants receive 2 weeks baseline; those with zero events receive one mandatory offer of a 2-week extension (capped at 4 weeks total), with extension acceptance entered prospectively at day-10 decision point to allow recruitment planning; reduces administrative volatility while permitting event-yield optimization for a subset.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between operational consistency and scientific data completeness, but the options underestimate the human burden: extended stays strain participant psychological resilience, increase attrition risk if stays exceed 4 weeks, and may worsen data quality in fatigued or demotivated participants. None of the options address participant adherence or morale degradation during long admissions.

levers.name: Technician Annotation Authority: Final Event Adjudication versus Dual-Rater Verification Gate
levers.consequences: Granting night technicians final event annotation authority (with dual-rater review as secondary audit) accelerates real-time decision-making, reduces next-day overhead, and empowers technicians to flag escalation PSG immediately based on their judgment. However, technician expertise variability introduces systematic bias early in data collection, and errors in event classification propagate to the event-triage tool training set, risking biased algorithm development. Strict dual-rater verification (all events scored by two independent raters offline, with technician flagging as input only) ensures methodological rigor and defensible scoring but delays event confirmation by 1–2 days, loses the opportunity for same-night PSG escalation, and increases cost and bottleneck if raters are bottlenecked.
options: Designate night technicians as primary event scorers with authority to flag escalation PSG; institute weekly inter-rater reliability spot-checks (dual-rater review of 10% of flagged events) to monitor technician drift, adjusting training and criteria as needed; privileges real-time responsiveness over pre-event rigor but requires robust technician competency management.

Enforce mandatory dual-rater consensus on all events before escalation PSG or final event classification; technician reports observations only, two independent offline raters provide scored judgment, and escalation is triggered only after rater agreement; guarantees methodological consistency but forfeits real-time escalation capability and requires sufficient rater availability.

Stratify rater authority by event confidence: technician high-confidence flags (clear motor cluster with arousal signature on dry-EEG and prolonged video movement) permit immediate escalation; borderline or ambiguous flags undergo dual-rater review before escalation decision; combines real-time responsiveness for unambiguous events with methodological gatekeeping for uncertain cases.
levers.review: This lever governs the boundary between operational expediency and methodological defensibility, but the options sidestep a deeper issue: technician expertise takes 3–6 months to mature, yet the facility must make escalation decisions from week one. None of the options propose ramp-up protocols, supervised learning periods, or explicit competency milestones before technicians gain independent judgment.

levers.name: Control Cohort Recruitment Scope: Matched Pairs versus Broader Population Sample
levers.consequences: Matched controls (age, sex, body mass, sleep schedule similar to cases) maximize statistical power for within-pair event-triage benchmarking and provide tightly controlled baseline false-positive rates for sensor validation. However, intensive matching slows control recruitment, constrains the external generalizability of sensor false-alarm baselines to broader populations, and ties control numbers to case availability. Broader population controls (unmatched, recruited independently to represent general adult sleep patterns) accelerate control accrual, reduce recruitment dependencies, and improve generalizability of false-alarm benchmarks to future real-world deployment—but introduce confounding variability (sleep-apnea prevalence, age spread, body size heterogeneity) that complicates event-triage tool training if the algorithm cannot distinguish control baseline variation from true parasomnia signal.
options: Recruit matched controls: for each enrolled NREM parasomnia participant, enroll one control matched on age (±5 years), sex, and body mass index (±3 kg/m²), monitoring for 2 weeks in identical residential conditions; maximizes power for within-pair sensor false-alarm benchmarking but extends recruitment timeline and risks incomplete matching if control availability lags case enrollment.

Enroll a broad unmatched control cohort (target 20–25 controls, representative of adult ages 25–60, both sexes, unrestricted BMI) recruited independently from sleep clinic outpatients and community via Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin; accelerates control accrual and improves external generalizability of sensor baseline norms but increases confounding variability in arousal patterns and false-positive rates.
