Implement stratified control recruitment with partial matching: controls are stratified by age decile and sex, with recruitment targets of 2–3 controls per stratum (n=20–30 total), ensuring age and sex coverage without strict pair-wise matching; balances recruitment agility with adequate stratification for downstream algorithmic control by case characteristics.
levers.review: This lever trades statistical purity against recruitment efficiency and generalizability, but the options miss the methodological reality: if control nocturnal arousal patterns are confounded by undiagnosed sleep apnea or circadian disorders, the event-triage tool will learn spurious signal-to-noise discrimination. None of the options discuss screening controls for occult sleep pathology or handling controls with incidental findings.

levers.name: Nightly Sensor Burden Distribution: Continuous Baseline Acquisition versus Scheduled Targeted Nights
levers.consequences: Continuous baseline acquisition (dry-EEG, mattress sensor, camera, microphone running every night for all participants) provides uninterrupted context for within-person variability, temporal clustering of events, and sleep-stage transitions, supporting rich longitudinal phenotyping. However, continuous deployment increases data volume (requiring larger storage, faster backup pipelines, longer annotation time), raises participant burden and privacy concerns (continuous video recording), and demands robust equipment maintenance and battery management across all 8 suites. Scheduled targeted nights (baseline sensors run only 2–3 nights per week, escalated to nightly after event detection) reduces data burden and privacy footprint but sacrifices the granular temporal map of episodic clustering, slows detection of rare multi-night patterns, and may miss events on non-monitoring nights.
options: Deploy dry-EEG, contact-free mattress sensors, ambient infrared camera, and microphone every night for all active participants for the entire stay duration, accepting continuous data volume and privacy implications; guarantees maximal within-person temporal coverage and supports rich clustering analysis but requires robust nightly annotation workflow and participant comfort with continuous ambient monitoring.

Operate baseline sensors (dry-EEG, mattress, microphone, ambient camera) on a rotating schedule of 3 nights per week per participant, with accelerated nightly monitoring triggered after technician-flagged event; reduces continuous data volume and privacy intrusiveness but risks missing rare multi-night patterns and introduces selection bias if events cluster on scheduled monitoring nights by chance.

Run dry-EEG and contact-free mattress sensors nightly for arousal and movement context, but limit infrared camera and microphone to 2–3 scheduled nights per week (increased to nightly after event detection) to reduce video privacy burden while maintaining physiological baseline; balances physiological continuity against privacy and annotation overhead.
levers.review: This lever trades continuous phenotypic fidelity against data management cost and participant privacy comfort, but the options do not address the hidden trade-off: selective monitoring nights may systematically over-detect events on filmed nights if participants are more hypervigilant when they know they are being recorded. None of the options propose blinding participants to monitoring schedules or assessing reactivity.

levers.name: Post-Study Data Retention and Ancillary Biospecimen Collection: Lean Repository versus Enriched Archive
levers.consequences: Lean repository approach (deposit only de-identified EEG and sensor streams in BIDS format; discard raw video; no biospecimens) minimizes storage cost, simplifies long-term legal obligations, and reduces participant re-consent burden for future secondary uses. However, it forgoes the opportunity to link parasomnia phenotypes to genetic, proteomic, or metabolic markers in future studies, limits richness of re-analysis if new automated scoring methods emerge, and reduces the facility's attractiveness to pharmaceutical partners or larger consortium efforts. Enriched archive (retain raw and processed EEG, video for approved secondary research, collect and bank saliva or blood for future biomarker studies) enables richer longitudinal science and cross-domain discovery but increases storage infrastructure cost, requires robust informed-consent language for future uses, and raises governance complexity if biospecimens are shared with external partners.
options: Adopt a minimalist data repository: de-identify and deposit only processed EEG montages and sensor streams in BIDS format to a recognized repository; retain raw video locally under access controls for a maximum of 5 years, then destroy; collect no biospecimens; minimizes storage, legal, and consent overhead but forecloses future biomarker studies and rich re-analysis.

Establish an enriched institutional archive: deposit de-identified processed EEG, raw and processed sensor streams, and secure de-identified video clips (key moments flagged by technician) in a university-controlled repository under conditional-access data use agreements; collect and bank saliva samples (DNA, proteins) for each participant with extended consent for genetic or metabolic studies; enables richer future research but requires governance infrastructure and long-term storage commitment.

Implement a hybrid archive with tiered retention: de-identify and publicly deposit processed EEG and sensor data in BIDS format; retain raw video and processed proprietary montages in a secure university repository with a 10-year retention mandate under restricted access agreements; collect saliva samples and bank with explicit participant consent for approved academic collaborators only, excluding commercial use without fresh consent.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between present scientific stewardship and future research opportunity, but the options do not adequately address the practical risk: biospecimen banking requires cryogenic infrastructure, regulatory compliance, and trained personnel—if these are absent at year one, building them later is costlier and slower. None of the options explicitly evaluate institutional capacity or discuss whether biobanking aligns with the facility's core mission of parasomnia phenotyping rather than becoming scope creep.

levers.name: Sleep-Onset Context Manipulation: Pharmacological Sleep Induction versus Naturalistic Circadian Entrainment
levers.consequences: Using short-acting sedatives (e.g., low-dose zopiclone) on arrival nights accelerates sleep onset and shortens the time to observable REM and NREM architecture, potentially increasing early event capture. However, sedative use alters intrinsic arousal thresholds and parasomnia susceptibility, introducing a confound that complicates phenotyping and limits generalizability to unsedated home sleep. Strict circadian entrainment (dark adaptation, scheduled lights-off, no sedation) preserves naturalistic physiology but extends the pre-event observation window and increases length-of-stay costs for slow sleepers.
options: Administer standardized low-dose sedation on participant arrival night only, then transition to naturalistic sleep across remaining nights, documenting the sedation effect in all analyses as a controlled confounder

Implement a 3–5 day circadian entrainment protocol prior to formal monitoring, with early-arrival accommodation in the residential unit to allow sleep-wake rhythm synchronization without pharmacological intervention

Randomize incoming participants to sedated versus unsedated-onset groups prospectively, creating two matched cohorts to quantify the magnitude of sedation-induced sleep architecture changes and parasomnia expression
levers.review: This lever balances ecological validity against capture speed, but the options do not address the practical ethics question: if sedation demonstrably increases early event yield, is withholding it from the unsedated group justifiable given limited recruitment capacity? None of the options propose transparent participant consent language that acknowledges this trade-off.

levers.name: Technician Staffing Schedule: Three-Person Continuous Coverage versus Singleton Night-Watch with On-Call Backup
levers.consequences: Three rotating technicians on each night ensure redundancy, rapid response to safety incidents, and real-time annotation of multiple simultaneous events across 8 suites. This model's overhead cost is approximately €180–200K annually in salary and social insurance. A singleton night-watch with on-call backup (two technicians covering three nights each per week, plus a rapid-response clinician on standby) reduces staffing costs by 35–40% but creates a single point of failure: a sole technician cannot simultaneously monitor safety, annotate events, and respond to medical episodes in multiple suites.
options: Deploy three rotating technicians every night across all 8 suites for the full 3-year operational period, accepting higher staffing cost in exchange for consistent safety margin and annotation throughput

Pilot a singleton-plus-on-call model for the first 12 months with early safety gates (incident reporting, event-miss audit, staff fatigue tracking), and escalate to three-person coverage if safety incidents exceed 2 per month or annotation delay exceeds 48 hours

Hire two full-time night technicians with a third contracted on-call position, staffed 5 nights per week with an on-site resident clinician (rotating MD or NP) available for clinical decisions on nights with lower expected occupancy
levers.review: This lever governs the core tension between cost containment and safety culture, but the options overlook the long-term burnout risk: even rotating three technicians will face cumulative fatigue and turnover if the role lacks advancement pathways or continued education funding. None of the options propose how to retain skilled staff beyond 3 years or transition to a sustainable post-pilot staffing model.

levers.name: Annotation Workflow Authority: Single-Technician Real-Time Flagging versus Delayed Consensus Screening Panel
levers.consequences: Real-time annotation by the night technician (who flags candidate parasomnia events and triggers PSG escalation if criteria are met) enables immediate clinical response and reduced revisit burden, but introduces subjective bias and inter-technician variability in event nomination. A delayed consensus panel (two independent sleep medicine physicians reviewing video/PSG 24–48 hours post-recording and adjudicating flagged events via standardized rubric) guarantees AASM-criterion adherence but adds 2–5 days of latency, delaying PSG escalation and potentially missing recurrent events during the same stay.
options: Empower each night technician to independently nominate candidate parasomnia events in real time using a laminated decision tree, trigger PSG escalation immediately upon nomination, and conduct blinded dual-rater scoring 1–2 weeks post-stay for research adjudication

Establish a daily remote morning review by a designated sleep physician who reviews overnight video and sensor data flagged by the technician, makes same-day clinical escalation decisions, and assigns events to dual-rater scoring

Implement a hybrid model where technicians flag low-confidence candidate events for morning review by the PI or on-call clinician (turnaround <12 hours), while high-confidence events (e.g., clear video-documented complex motor behavior matching pre-study witness account) trigger immediate PSG escalation without waiting for physician review
levers.review: This lever balances rapid clinical responsiveness against diagnostic rigor, but the options do not adequately address annotation burden escalation: as participant volume increases from 15–20 to 30–35, the number of flagged events will multiply, and dual-rater availability may become the bottleneck. None propose how to scale the dual-rater pool or integrate semi-automated pre-screening to reduce manual reviewer load.

levers.name: Early-Discharge Threshold for Low-Yield Participants: Hard Protocol Ceiling versus Extended Observation with Phenotypic Reassessment
levers.consequences: Discharging participants after 2 weeks without a captured event saves approximately €2,100 per participant in accommodation and technician time, preserving budget for additional enrollments. However, removing low-yield participants introduces selection bias (faster, shallower sleepers or those with genuinely infrequent episodes are overrepresented in the discharge group), skewing the longitudinal phenotyping cohort. The current protocol permits extension up to 10 weeks with potential for 20% unproductive admissions; stricter early-discharge policies reduce the unproductive allowance but risk losing participants whose episodes cluster later in the stay.
options: Establish an absolute 2-week maximum stay (or 3 weeks if one event has been captured) regardless of ongoing monitoring interest, with post-discharge phone follow-up at 1, 3, and 6 months to capture delayed phenotypic clustering and stratify the null-yield group by eventual symptom trajectory

Implement a staged protocol: 2 weeks of baseline monitoring followed by a blinded reassessment interview (with bed partner if available) to re-examine symptom frequency and triggers; if interview suggests ongoing high likelihood, extend stay for an additional 2–3 weeks with modified environmental or sleep-timing interventions targeted at participant's reported trigger patterns

Admit all participants with confirmed pre-admission parasomnia diagnosis for a fixed 4-week minimum stay, allowing at least two complete sleep cycles for potential within-person temporal clustering, with discharge extensions considered only after the 4-week gate is reached
levers.review: This lever governs the trade-off between statistical efficiency and sample representativeness, but the options do not address the ethical implication: extending stays for low-yield participants may create expectancy pressure (both on staff and participants) for events to occur, subtly incentivizing over-interpretation of ambiguous arousal bursts as parasomnia candidates. None propose how to monitor and manage this form of subtle observer bias.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Scoring Protocol: Strict AASM Criterion Adherence versus Pragmatic Consensus Rubric
levers.consequences: AASM-criterion-only scoring (both raters independently apply published polysomnography scoring rules without modification) ensures external comparability and aligns with clinical standards, but the criteria were designed for single-night diagnostic PSG and may not account for within-person variability, recurrent subclinical arousal patterns, or subtle morphological differences that characterize longitudinal monitoring. A locally developed consensus rubric (co-authored by the two independent raters, documented in a technical supplement) can capture nuanced longitudinal phenomena and reduce false negatives but sacrifices generalizability and introduces the appearance of ad-hoc rule-making that may be challenged in peer review.
options: Score all 50–70 participants using strict published AASM criteria, with a separate exploratory analysis layer that documents borderline or ambiguous events flagged by either rater and summarizes their characteristics; report AASM-criterion adherence as the primary results and exploratory findings as supplementary

Develop a hybrid rubric during the pilot phase (first 15–20 participants) in collaboration with two independent sleep physicians external to the project, pre-register the rubric, and apply it uniformly to all 70 participants, with transparent documentation of where the rubric deviates from or extends published AASM guidance

Train both independent raters using didactic video examples (parasomnia vs. confusional arousal vs. normal arousal boundary cases) and conduct a structured calibration scoring session (10–15 blinded reference events) before they score study data, then report inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa and ICC) as the primary validity metric alongside event counts
levers.review: This lever addresses the tension between standardization and fidelity, but the options overlook the reputational risk: if the study is later found to have departed from AASM criteria without preregistration, the entire dataset may be discounted by the field. None of the options propose prospective publication of the scoring protocol or early expert review by the AASM polysomnography task force.

levers.name: Sensor Failure and Data Loss Contingency: Redundant Recording Across All Participants versus Tiered Redundancy by Participant Risk Profile
levers.consequences: Recording all participants on dual independent sensor streams (e.g., two separate EEG headband units plus the PSG montage on enhanced nights) ensures no single hardware failure loses the physiological record, but doubles equipment cost (approximately €80–100K in headbands plus redundant storage) and increases data management complexity without increasing the probability of actual event capture (redundancy protects against sensor dropout, not against event rarity). Tiered redundancy (dual sensors only for participants with low baseline event frequency or those on extended stays; single sensors for high-yielding participants) reduces cost but introduces operational complexity and creates a per-participant decision point that may be biased by early event yield.
options: Deploy dual independent EEG headband systems on all participants from day one, synchronized via network time protocol, with automatic failover annotation in the data pipeline if one stream drops; report data completeness as a primary quality metric

Implement single-sensor baseline recording for all participants, but automatically escalate to dual-sensor backup recording for any participant whose first 5 nights produce zero flagged events, or whose initial event-capture rate falls below the cohort median by day 10

Assign redundancy status at admission based on pre-study frequency history (participants reporting <1 episode per month receive dual sensors; ≥1 per month receive single) with protocol-specified criteria for upgrades during the stay if low yield persists
levers.review: This lever balances cost against data robustness, but the options do not address the subtle cognitive bias they introduce: staff may unconsciously invest more annotation effort in high-redundancy (high-risk) participants, creating false-negative bias in low-redundancy (presumed high-yield) participants. None of the options propose blinded annotation schemes or periodic random re-review of single-sensor data.

levers.name: Facility Capacity Activation Timing: Phased Expansion to 10 Suites at Month 12 versus Maintaining 8-Suite Steady State through Year Three
levers.consequences: Expanding from 8 to 10 active suites during year two requires approximately €150–180K in additional renovation, equipment, and one incremental technician FTE (offset by one additional participant per month generating €800 in annual revenue). Expansion shortens average time-to-event by 20–25% due to larger cohort size, but introduces operational risk: the pilot phase (first 12 months) may uncover staffing, safety, or annotation bottlenecks that cannot be corrected once the 10-suite footprint is activated. Remaining at 8 suites preserves budget flexibility, reduces operational complexity, and allows 50–60 participants over 3 years; expansion enables 65–75 participants but requires successful resolution of hidden year-one challenges.
options: Commit to 8-suite operations through month 36, using freed capital (€150K+) for enhanced technician training, redundant equipment, and a post-hoc validation cohort recruited in year three to test the semi-automated event-triage model on independent data

Expand to 10 suites at month 13 (after 12 months of pilot operations and external advisory board approval), contingent on meeting three go-live gates: zero serious safety incidents, participant enrollment on pace (≥12 cumulative admits), and annotation throughput sustainable within 48 hours

Execute a phased expansion: activate a 9th suite at month 12 with the incumbent technician team and concurrent hiring of one additional FTE; do not open the 10th suite unless event-capture rates remain above the pre-study assumption (70–80% with ≥1 event per stay) after 20 total admissions
levers.review: This lever determines the long-term resource envelope and success probability, but the options do not adequately weigh the hidden costs of over-expansion: adding suites increases the minimum safe occupancy threshold required to justify staffing, which paradoxically makes recruitment pressure more acute if enrollment slows. None of the options propose how to right-size staffing dynamically if cohort size plateaus unexpectedly.

system_prompt:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     - "Prioritizing speed over reliability carries hidden costs: none of the options address rollback complexity."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration

Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
